title: Cash flow
summary: Cash is the fuel a business runs on, and a firm that runs out of it fails even while its profit-and-loss account still shows a profit. This lesson works through the whole of syllabus 3.7: the difference between cash flow and profit (and why a profitable business can still go under); the cash flow forecast and its five moving parts — opening balance, cash inflows, cash outflows, net cash flow and closing balance; working capital and the working-capital cycle; the causes of liquidity and cash-flow problems; and the strategies used to improve cash flow, from managing receivables and payables to reducing outflows, seeking finance and sale and leaseback. Because 3.7 is a quantitative topic, the model answer is a cash-flow CALCULATION set out exactly the way our marking engine awards Business Management marks — M for the correct method and A for the correct accuracy figure, with follow-through down the columns — so you can see how the numbers, not a memorised definition, earn the marks.
learningObjectives: Explain the difference between cash flow and profit, and why a profitable business can still fail through a lack of liquidity.
Construct and interpret a cash flow forecast using opening balance, cash inflows, cash outflows, net cash flow and closing balance.
Define working capital and explain the working-capital cycle.
Analyse the causes of liquidity and cash-flow problems.
Evaluate strategies to improve cash flow — managing receivables and payables, reducing outflows, seeking finance and sale and leaseback — and apply them to a specific business.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Fuel in the tank, not miles on the map
simpleExplanation.summary: Cash flow is the movement of money into and out of a business over a period of time. Profit is what is left when you compare the revenue earned with the costs incurred. They are not the same thing, and the gap between them is where profitable businesses quietly run out of money and die.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Picture a long road trip. Profit is the distance you plan to cover — 1,000 miles to a destination you can already see on the map. Cash is the fuel in the tank right now. You can be heading for a huge, profitable destination, but if the tank runs dry after 100 miles you are stranded at the roadside, however promising the map looks. Managing cash flow is making sure there is enough fuel in the tank at every stage of the journey, not just enough distance planned on the map. A business that forgets this can be 'profitable' all the way to insolvency.
steps: Start with the opening balance — the cash the business already has at the beginning of the period.
Add up every source of money coming in during the period (cash inflows): cash sales, payments from customers who bought on credit, loans, owner's capital.
Add up every payment going out during the period (cash outflows): wages, rent, payments to suppliers, loan repayments, buying equipment.
Take outflows away from inflows to get net cash flow — a positive figure is a surplus, a negative figure is a deficit.
Add net cash flow to the opening balance to get the closing balance, which is carried forward to become next period's opening balance.

flashcards.back: The movement of money into (inflows) and out of (outflows) a business over a period of time. It measures liquidity — the ability to pay short-term debts as they fall due — not profitability.
Money flowing INTO the business during a period: cash sales, payments received from debtors (accounts receivable), bank loans, and capital introduced by owners or investors.
Money flowing OUT of the business during a period: wages, rent, utility bills, payments to suppliers (accounts payable), loan repayments, interest and purchases of fixed assets.
Total cash inflows minus total cash outflows over a period. Net cash flow = total inflows − total outflows. A positive figure is a cash surplus; a negative figure is a cash deficit.
The cash the business holds at the START of a period. It equals the previous period's closing balance, which is what links the months of a forecast together.
The cash the business holds at the END of a period. Closing balance = opening balance + net cash flow. It is carried forward as next period's opening balance.
A forward-looking estimate of a firm's cash inflows, cash outflows and cash balances over a future period, usually month by month, used to spot cash shortages before they happen.

Profit = revenue − costs, recorded when a sale is made and a cost is incurred (even on credit) and including non-cash items like depreciation. Cash flow records only the actual movement of money when it is received or paid. A business can be profitable yet have no cash.
The ability of a business to pay its short-term debts as they fall due. A business with poor liquidity cannot meet its bills even if it is profitable on paper.
When a business can no longer pay its debts as they fall due. It is the end-point of a severe, prolonged liquidity problem — and it is what forces profitable-on-paper firms to close.
The finance available for the day-to-day running of a business. Working capital = current assets − current liabilities. Too little working capital is a leading cause of liquidity problems.

The time it takes for cash spent on inputs to flow back to the business as cash from customers: cash → buy stock → sell stock (often on credit) → collect from debtors → cash again. The longer the cycle, the more cash is tied up.
Money owed TO the business by customers who bought on credit. Collecting it faster (tighter credit control) speeds up cash inflows.
Money owed BY the business to suppliers who sold on credit. Negotiating longer payment terms delays cash outflows and eases short-term cash flow.

Expanding too fast and taking on more orders than the firm can fund, so it runs short of working capital to pay for stock and wages. A common cause of cash-flow crises in otherwise profitable, growing firms.

Selling a fixed asset the business owns (e.g. property or machinery) to a finance company and then leasing it straight back. It releases a large lump of cash while the firm carries on using the asset, though it must now pay rent on it.

quickCheck.prompt: A business makes a sale of $10,000 on credit; the customer will pay in 60 days. What is the immediate effect?
options: Both profit and cash rise by $10,000 now
Profit rises now, but cash does not rise until the customer actually pays
Cash rises now, but profit is unaffected
Neither profit nor cash changes until the customer pays
quickCheck.answer: Profit rises now, but cash does not rise until the customer actually pays

quickCheck.prompt: Opening balance is $8,000 and net cash flow for the month is −$3,000. What is the closing balance?
A firm has current assets of $40,000 and current liabilities of $25,000. What is its working capital?
In March a business has total inflows of $18,000 and total outflows of $22,000. Its net cash flow is:
Which statement correctly explains how a profitable business can still fail?
options: It cannot — profit guarantees the business has cash
Its customers pay on credit and its bills fall due first, so it runs out of cash to pay them even though sales are profitable
Profit and cash are the same, so failure means it was never profitable
It fails only if it stops making a profit
quickCheck.answer: Its customers pay on credit and its bills fall due first, so it runs out of cash to pay them even though sales are profitable

faq.q: Can a profitable business really go bankrupt?
faq.a: Yes, and it happens often. Profit is earned the moment a sale is made, even on credit, but the cash may not arrive for 30, 60 or 90 days. Meanwhile wages, rent and suppliers still have to be paid now. If a growing, profitable business sells on credit and its bills fall due before its customers pay, it runs out of cash — becomes insolvent — and is forced to close, however healthy its profit-and-loss account looks. Profit is an opinion recorded over a period; cash is a fact you need on the day the bill lands.

faq.q: Is a negative net cash flow always a bad sign?
faq.a: Not always. A one-off negative net cash flow can be perfectly healthy if it is planned — for example a month when the firm buys a large batch of stock or a new machine that will generate inflows later. What matters is whether the closing balance stays viable and whether the negatives are temporary and planned. A persistent, unplanned run of negative net cash flow that drives the closing balance below zero is a serious liquidity warning.

faq.q: What is the difference between working capital and cash flow?
faq.a: They are related but not the same. Working capital is a snapshot at one moment — current assets minus current liabilities — measuring the funds available for day-to-day operations. Cash flow is a movement over a period — the money flowing in and out. Weak cash flow drains working capital over time; too little working capital means a firm cannot absorb a cash-flow shock. Examiners like students who keep the two ideas distinct: one is a stock (a level), the other is a flow (a movement).

faq.q: How does managing receivables and payables improve cash flow?
faq.a: Receivables (debtors) are money owed TO the business; payables (creditors) are money the business owes to suppliers. To improve cash flow you want cash IN sooner and cash OUT later. So you tighten credit control to collect from debtors faster — shorter credit periods, prompt-payment discounts, chasing overdue invoices — while negotiating LONGER credit terms from your own suppliers so you pay them later. Done together this shortens the gap the business has to fund and eases the working-capital cycle.

faq.q: What is sale and leaseback and when would a firm use it?
faq.a: The business sells a fixed asset it owns — typically property or expensive machinery — to a finance company for a lump sum of cash, then immediately leases the same asset back and carries on using it. It is a way to unlock cash tied up in assets to solve a liquidity problem or fund expansion, without stopping operations. The trade-off is that the firm no longer owns the asset and must now pay rent on it, so over the long run it can cost more and it loses an asset it could have borrowed against later.

sections.content: You have probably heard the phrase 'cash is king'. It captures one of the hardest truths in business: a company can be profitable, growing and full of orders, and still collapse — because if it runs out of cash to pay its wages, its suppliers or its loan instalments, it becomes insolvent and is forced to close. This lesson covers the whole of syllabus 3.7 — the difference between cash flow and profit and why the gap between them kills profitable firms, the cash flow forecast and its five parts, working capital and the working-capital cycle, the causes of liquidity problems, and the strategies used to fix them. Because 3.7 is a quantitative topic, the model answer is a cash-flow CALCULATION worked exactly the way our marking engine awards marks — a method mark for setting the calculation up correctly and an accuracy mark for the right figure — so you can see precisely where the numbers earn credit.
Cash flow is not profit

The single most important idea in this topic is that cash flow and profit are different things. Profit is calculated as revenue minus costs, and it is recorded when a sale is made and a cost is incurred — even if no money has actually changed hands yet. Cash flow records only the real movement of money: cash counts as an inflow when it is genuinely received, and as an outflow when it is genuinely paid. A firm can make a highly profitable sale on credit, so its profit rises today, while not a single dollar of cash has entered the business — the cash only arrives when the customer settles the invoice weeks later. In the meantime the firm still has to pay wages, rent and suppliers in cash. That timing gap is exactly how a profitable business runs out of money and fails.

items: **Timing:** profit is recorded when revenue is earned and costs are incurred (accrual basis); cash flow is recorded when money is actually received or paid (cash basis).
**Credit sales:** a credit sale raises profit now but raises cash only when the customer pays — the source of most cash-flow crises.
**Non-cash items:** profit includes non-cash costs such as depreciation; cash flow does not.
**Capital expenditure:** buying a machine is a huge cash outflow now, but it reduces profit only gradually through depreciation.
**Loans:** receiving a loan is a big cash inflow but not profit; repaying the principal is a cash outflow but not a cost (only the interest is a cost).

sections.content: This is why a profitable business can still fail: liquidity, not profitability, is what keeps the doors open day to day. Liquidity is the ability to pay short-term debts as they fall due. A firm whose customers all pay on 60-day credit while its own bills fall due in 30 days is chronically short of cash — and when it cannot pay, it becomes insolvent, regardless of how much profit its accounts report.
The cash flow forecast

A cash flow forecast is a forward-looking estimate of the money a business expects to flow in and out, usually set out month by month. Its purpose is to spot cash shortages before they happen, so managers can arrange an overdraft or delay a payment in good time rather than being caught out. Every forecast is built from the same five parts, in the same order: it starts with the cash already held, adds everything received, subtracts everything paid out, works out the net movement, and arrives at the cash held at the end — which then becomes the start of the next month.

items: **Opening balance:** the cash held at the start of the month (equal to last month's closing balance).
**Cash inflows:** all money received during the month — cash sales, debtor receipts, loans, owner's capital.
**Cash outflows:** all money paid during the month — wages, rent, supplier payments, loan repayments, asset purchases.
**Net cash flow:** total inflows minus total outflows for the month.
**Closing balance:** opening balance plus net cash flow — carried forward as next month's opening balance.

sections.content: Net cash flow = total cash inflows − total cash outflows
Closing balance = opening balance + net cash flow

sections.question: Worked example 1 — building a forecast. 'The Corner Slice', a new pizza takeaway, opens with $4,000 in the bank. A bank loan of $10,000 arrives in January. Forecast cash sales are: Jan $6,000, Feb $7,000, Mar $8,500. Monthly costs are rent $1,200, wages $2,500, and raw materials of 30% of that month's sales. Construct the cash flow forecast for the three months.
sections.solution:
**Step 1 — raw material costs (30% of sales):**
- Jan: 30% × $6,000 = $1,800
- Feb: 30% × $7,000 = $2,100
- Mar: 30% × $8,500 = $2,550

**Step 2 — the forecast:**

| ($) | **January** | **February** | **March** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Opening balance** | 4,000 | 14,500 | 15,700 |
| Cash sales | 6,000 | 7,000 | 8,500 |
| Bank loan | 10,000 | 0 | 0 |
| **Total inflows** | **16,000** | **7,000** | **8,500** |
| Rent | 1,200 | 1,200 | 1,200 |
| Wages | 2,500 | 2,500 | 2,500 |
| Raw materials | 1,800 | 2,100 | 2,550 |
| **Total outflows** | **5,500** | **5,800** | **6,250** |
| **Net cash flow** | 10,500 | 1,200 | 2,250 |
| **Closing balance** | **14,500** | **15,700** | **17,950** |

**Working:**
- Jan net cash flow: $16,000 − $5,500 = $10,500; closing: $4,000 + $10,500 = $14,500
- Feb net cash flow: $7,000 − $5,800 = $1,200; closing: $14,500 + $1,200 = $15,700
- Mar net cash flow: $8,500 − $6,250 = $2,250; closing: $15,700 + $2,250 = $17,950

Notice how each month's closing balance becomes the next month's opening balance — that carry-forward is the spine of the whole forecast, and dropping it is the most common structural error.

sections.content: Working capital and the working-capital cycle

Working capital is the finance available for the day-to-day running of the business — the money it uses to pay wages and suppliers while it waits for customers to pay. It is calculated as current assets minus current liabilities. Current assets are things expected to turn into cash within a year (cash itself, stock, and debtors); current liabilities are debts due within a year (creditors, overdrafts, short-term loans). If current liabilities exceed current assets, the firm has negative working capital and is likely to struggle to pay its bills.
Working capital = current assets − current liabilities

The working-capital cycle is the time it takes for cash spent on inputs to come back as cash from customers. The business pays cash to buy stock, holds the stock, sells it (often on credit), waits for the debtors to pay, and only then has cash again — ready to start the loop over. The longer this cycle, the more cash is tied up in stock and unpaid invoices, and the more working capital the firm needs to bridge the gap. A firm that holds stock for a long time and gives customers generous credit while paying its own suppliers quickly has a long, cash-hungry cycle; shortening it (less stock, faster collection, slower payment to suppliers) frees up cash.

items: **Current assets:** cash, stock (inventory) and debtors (accounts receivable) — expected to become cash within a year.
**Current liabilities:** creditors (accounts payable), overdrafts and other debts due within a year.
**The cycle:** cash → buy stock → sell stock (often on credit) → collect from debtors → cash again.
**Longer cycle = more cash tied up:** slow-selling stock and slow-paying customers lengthen the cycle and drain working capital.
**Shortening the cycle frees cash:** hold less stock, collect from debtors faster, pay suppliers later.

sections.content: Causes of liquidity and cash-flow problems

A single negative month is not a crisis, especially if it was planned. But a persistent negative net cash flow, or a closing balance that turns negative, is a genuine liquidity problem with identifiable causes. The most common are overtrading (expanding faster than the firm can fund), poor credit control (letting customers take too long to pay), holding too much stock (tying cash up on the shelf), heavy one-off spending on fixed assets, seasonal demand that concentrates inflows in a few months, and simply having costs that are too high for the revenue coming in. Unexpected shocks — a big customer paying late or going bust, an economic downturn cutting sales — can tip an already-tight firm into insolvency.

items: **Overtrading:** growing so fast that orders outrun the working capital needed to fund stock and wages.
**Poor credit control:** giving customers long credit or failing to chase overdue debtors, so cash arrives too slowly.
**Too much stock:** cash locked up in unsold inventory that could be financing operations.
**Heavy capital expenditure:** a large one-off payment for equipment or premises draining the cash balance.
**Seasonality and external shocks:** inflows bunched into a few months, or a downturn or bad debt hitting an already-tight cash position.

sections.content: Strategies to improve cash flow

The strategies to improve cash flow all do one of two things: bring cash in sooner, or push cash out later. Managing receivables means collecting from customers faster — shorter credit periods, prompt-payment discounts, credit checks and firm chasing of overdue invoices — or even selling the debts to a factoring company for immediate cash. Managing payables means the opposite for your own bills: negotiating longer credit terms with suppliers so cash leaves the business later. Reducing outflows means cutting or delaying spending — trimming costs, postponing non-essential purchases, or leasing assets rather than buying them so a large one-off outflow becomes small monthly payments. Seeking finance means arranging an overdraft, a short-term loan or new investment to cover a gap. And sale and leaseback releases cash tied up in an asset the firm owns by selling it and leasing it straight back. Each has a cost, so the right choice depends on the specific problem.

items: **Manage receivables:** shorter credit terms, prompt-payment discounts, credit checks, chasing debtors, or debt factoring — speeds cash IN. Cost: discounts and lost sales if terms are too tight.
**Manage payables:** negotiate longer credit from suppliers — delays cash OUT. Cost: may forfeit early-payment discounts or strain supplier goodwill.
**Reduce outflows:** cut or postpone spending, and lease rather than buy assets to avoid large lump-sum outflows. Cost: leasing is dearer over time; cuts may harm the business.
**Seek finance:** overdraft, short-term loan or new investment to bridge a gap. Cost: interest, and dilution of ownership if new investors come in.
**Sale and leaseback:** sell a fixed asset and lease it back for an immediate cash lump sum while still using it. Cost: loss of ownership and ongoing rent.

sections.content: In Paper 2 you are usually given a cash flow forecast to complete or analyse. Two habits win marks. First, when you calculate, show the method — write 'net cash flow = inflows − outflows = 18,000 − 22,000 = −$4,000' rather than just landing a number, because the method earns a mark even if a figure slips. Second, when you suggest a strategy, tie it to the specific cause you identified in the data: if the problem is a $12,000 one-off equipment payment, recommend leasing or a loan to spread it, not a generic 'chase debtors'. Matching the fix to the diagnosed cause is where application marks live.

sections.question: Worked example 2 — diagnosing and fixing a deficit. The forecast for 'StyleMe Ltd', a clothing retailer, shows a negative closing balance of −$1,500 in October. October figures: opening balance $2,000; cash sales $15,000; receipts from debtors $5,000; payment for new shop fittings $12,000; stock purchases $8,000; wages and rent $3,500. Identify one cause of the cash-flow problem and recommend a suitable strategy. [6]
sections.solution:
**Step 1 — verify the figures.**
- Total inflows = $15,000 + $5,000 = $20,000
- Total outflows = $12,000 + $8,000 + $3,500 = $23,500
- Net cash flow = $20,000 − $23,500 = −$3,500
- Closing balance = $2,000 + (−$3,500) = −$1,500 ✓ (matches the stated figure)

**Step 2 — identify a cause.**
The clearest cause is the one-off $12,000 payment for new shop fittings. This single item of capital expenditure is more than half of October's total outflows and is what pushes the month into a $3,500 deficit and the closing balance below zero. Without it, net cash flow would have been $20,000 − $11,500 = +$8,500.

**Step 3 — recommend a suitable strategy.**
Instead of paying the full $12,000 in cash at once, StyleMe Ltd should have financed the fittings differently — for example a bank loan or a leasing agreement. This spreads the cost into smaller monthly payments rather than a single lump, turning a large one-off outflow into manageable instalments and keeping the October closing balance positive. Because the diagnosed cause is a lump-sum capital cost, spreading that cost is the fix that directly matches the problem — a more precise answer than simply 'chase debtors', which would not have prevented this particular deficit.

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Treating cash flow and profit as the same thing** — a profitable business can still run out of cash. Confusing the two is the single most penalised error in this topic; profit is revenue − costs on an accrual basis, cash flow is the real movement of money.

**Getting the closing-balance formula wrong** — closing balance = opening balance + net cash flow. Adding net cash flow to the wrong figure, or forgetting that a NEGATIVE net cash flow is ADDED (which lowers the balance), loses accuracy marks.

**Breaking the carry-forward** — each month's closing balance must become the next month's opening balance. Restarting every month from zero or from the original opening balance corrupts the whole forecast.
**Mishandling negatives** — writing a −$3,000 net cash flow but then adding $3,000 to the opening balance. A cash deficit reduces the balance; keep the sign consistent all the way down the column.

**Confusing working capital with cash flow** — working capital (current assets − current liabilities) is a snapshot level at one moment; cash flow is a movement over a period. Do not use one term for the other.

**Putting non-cash items in a cash flow forecast** — depreciation is a cost that reduces profit but is NOT a cash outflow, so it never appears in a cash flow forecast. Likewise a loan received is a cash inflow but is not revenue.

**Recommending a strategy that does not match the diagnosed cause** — suggesting 'chase debtors' when the problem is a one-off equipment purchase earns little credit. The fix must address the specific cause you identified in the data.

**Not showing the method** — landing a final number with no working means that if one figure is wrong, there is no method mark to fall back on. Always show inflows − outflows and opening + net cash flow explicitly.

sections.content: Model answer — a calculation, marked the way our engine marks it

Because 3.7 is a quantitative topic, the marks come from the numbers, not from a definition. Business Management calculation questions are marked by M/A conventions: an M (method) mark is awarded for setting the calculation up correctly — the right structure and operation — and an A (accuracy) mark is awarded for the correct final figure. Crucially, the marking is follow-through (the 'own-figure rule'): if you make one error early but carry your own figure correctly through the rest of the calculation, you still earn the later method marks. Watch how the marks below attach to each step — the method for the structure, the accuracy for the figure — and how a wrong opening figure carried correctly downward is still credited.

sections.question: Worked example 3 (the model answer). A business has an opening cash balance of $5,000. In January cash inflows are $20,000 and cash outflows are $23,000. Calculate the net cash flow and the closing balance, and state what the figure means for the business. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
Net cash flow = total inflows − total outflows = $20,000 − $23,000 = **−$3,000** (a cash deficit for January).

Closing balance = opening balance + net cash flow = $5,000 + (−$3,000) = **$2,000**.

What it means: January ran at a cash deficit — the business spent $3,000 more than it received — so its cash reserve fell from $5,000 to $2,000. It is still solvent and holds positive cash, but if this negative net cash flow continues the reserve will be exhausted within about two more months and the firm would face a liquidity problem, unable to pay its bills.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks** — this is a calculation, so marks are split into method (M, for the correct approach) and accuracy (A, for the correct figure), with follow-through applied down the columns:

- **M1 — net cash flow method:** awarded for subtracting outflows from inflows ($20,000 − $23,000). The correct structure earns this mark even if the arithmetic slips.
- **A1 — net cash flow accuracy:** awarded for the correct figure, −$3,000, shown as a negative (a deficit). Writing $3,000 without the minus sign forfeits this accuracy mark.
- **M1 — closing-balance method:** awarded for adding net cash flow to the opening balance ($5,000 + net cash flow). This is a follow-through mark: if a candidate wrongly found net cash flow as, say, −$2,000 but then correctly computed $5,000 + (−$2,000), they still earn this method mark for the correct structure.
- **A1 — closing-balance accuracy:** awarded for the correct figure, $2,000, consistent with the earlier figures.

The interpretation ('a deficit; cash reserve falling') secures the full accuracy marks by showing the figure is understood, not just produced. The engine rejects an answer that states $2,000 with no working, because there is then no method to credit if a figure is wrong — the whole value of M/A marking is that showing the method protects you.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR calculation this same way — an M mark for the right method (inflows − outflows, opening + net cash flow) and an A mark for the right figure, with follow-through if you carry your own earlier figure through correctly. So always SHOW your working: write the subtraction and the addition out in full, keep the minus sign on a deficit, and add a sentence saying what the closing balance means for the business. Method plus a stated meaning is how you bank all four marks even if one number slips.
Where this leads

Cash flow underpins the rest of the finance unit. The forecast you build here feeds directly into sources of finance (3.1), where the choice between an overdraft, a loan, leasing and sale and leaseback is really a choice about managing cash. Working capital connects to the final accounts and liquidity ratios that measure it, and the profit-versus-cash distinction is the foundation for profitability and investment appraisal later in the course. Master the habit built here — set the calculation out clearly, show the method, keep the signs right, and say what the figure means for the business — and you have the template that earns marks on every quantitative question in Business Management.

sections.label: Get a Paper 2 cash-flow question marked: calculate net cash flow and the closing balance, then explain what the figures mean for the business
